title: Countries with adoption suspensions or restrictions - Canada.ca
content:
## Countries with adoption suspensions or restrictions
Adoption rules vary by country and region. Some countries place limits on international adoptions or don’t allow them at all. Before you begin, contact the appropriate adoption authority in your province or territory. They will help you understand which countries you can currently adopt from.
Examples of restrictions:
- Some Canadian provinces and territories have suspended adoption from certain countries.
- Some countries only allow adoption by relatives.
- Some countries don’t allow international adoption.
- Some countries allow legal guardianship under their laws, but not adoption. Cases involving guardianship can’t be processed as adoptions because guardianship doesn’t meet the internationally accepted definition of an adoption (which creates a permanent, legal parent­child relationship).
Don’t adopt during a crisis
A crisis (such as a war or natural disaster) often separates children from their families.
In these situations, you shouldn’t adopt a child because
- reuniting the child with their parents or family must take priority
- it’s difficult to confirm if a child is actually available for adoption
For more information, check the statement from UNICEF (United Nations Children’s Fund) .

## Countries with a suspension on adoptions
Some Canadian provinces and territories (PTs) have suspended adoptions from these countries:
- Burundi (Quebec only)
- Haiti (all PTs except Alberta) Temporary
- Ukraine (all PTs) Temporary
- Vietnam (Quebec only)
Due to the current state of emergency and civil unrest in Haiti, all Canadian provinces and territories, except Alberta, have temporarily stopped accepting new applications for intercountry adoptions from Haiti. If you’re a resident of Alberta, contact the Central Authority of Alberta to find out if you meet the requirements to proceed with an adoption from Haiti.

## Countries that restrict international adoptions
- China (some exceptions apply)
- Democratic Republic of Congo (except adoption by relatives or family members)

## Countries that don’t allow adoption under their laws
IRCC has received a notification from the Ministry of Civil Affairs of the People’s Republic of China advising that effective August 28, 2024, China will no longer consider applications for adoptions by non-Chinese nationals. The only exception is for “adoptions by foreigners of stepchildren and children of collateral blood relatives within the third generation”.
IRCC is working closely with contacts in China to get more information about these changes.
Kenyan law only allows intercountry adoptions where the adopting parent or parents meet 1 of the following conditions:
- They’re Kenyan nationals with dual citizenship.
- They’re Kenyan nationals, whether or not they live in Kenya.
- They aren’t Kenyan nationals but are biologically related to the child.
- They were formerly Kenyan nationals who lost their nationality due to the laws of the current country.
On December 14, 2023, the Czech Republic confirmed that they
- restricted the number of countries they work with
- would no longer process intercountry adoptions with Canada
In January 2018, the Ethiopian Government changed a law to ban all intercountry adoptions.
Canada is aware that some people in other countries have adopted relatives from Ethiopia. However, the Ethiopian Government has confirmed to Canada that the intercountry adoption ban remains in effect .
Russia currently prohibits the adoption of Russian children
- by single applicants who are citizens of countries that recognize same-sex marriage
- by couples from all countries that recognize same-sex marriage
- by citizens of countries where gender transitioning is legal
- by couples from all countries that do not have a bilateral agreement on intercountry adoption with Russia
- from all countries that allow the re-adoption of Russian children without the oversight of Russia’s adoption authority (in cases where the original adoption fails)
This effectively bans Canadians from adopting Russian children.

## Other affected countries
You may not be able to adopt from certain countries if they don’t meet the requirements of the Hague Convention including the following:
- The child must be legally available for adoption.
- The adoption must create a legal parent-child relationship between the child and the adoptive parents.
The following provinces and territories have suspended adoptions from India under the Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act:
- Newfoundland and Labrador
- Northwest Territories
- Prince Edward Island
All provinces and territories still allow adoptions under the Indian Juvenile Justice Act (PDF, 226 KB) , which complies with the Hague Convention.

### Give feedback about this page
Thank you for your feedback.
